title: Q1 slowdown, CHP crisis deepens, Ankara bets on energy corridors
subhead: Turkey's GDP growth decelerated to 2.5% as the Iran war shock hit exports and industry, while the opposition CHP remains in turmoil after a court-ordered leadership change and a police raid on party headquarters. Meanwhile, Ankara unveiled plans for a TANAP-style electricity corridor with Azerbaijan and pushed for a bigger role in European defense ahead of the NATO Ankara Summit.

sections.heading: Q1 GDP slows to 2.5% as Iran war shock hits exports and industry
sections.body:
Turkey's economy expanded 2.5% year-on-year in Q1, down from 3.4% the previous quarter, marking a third consecutive slowdown, according to TurkStat data released Monday. Quarter-on-quarter growth was just 0.1%, well below the 0.3% consensus. The industrial sector contracted 0.8%, and exports of goods and services fell 12.7% year-on-year — the clearest signal yet that the Iran war's disruption of trade routes and energy markets is feeding through into real economic activity.

Treasury and Finance Minister Mehmet Simsek acknowledged that 'global uncertainties and the weak outlook among our trading partners, together with net external demand, limited growth,' posting on X. National income has exceeded $1.6 trillion, he said, with 23 consecutive quarters of uninterrupted growth. Bright spots included information and communication (up 9.5%), agriculture (up 4.6% after last year's drought-related decline), and construction (up 3.2%).

Consumer prices rose 32.37% annually in April, with the CBRT raising its end-2026 interim inflation target to 24% from 16%. ING analysts warned the outlook 'may translate into a more marked slowdown in the second half of the year,' though a potential Iran war resolution 'could help alleviate the current downside pressures.' Domestic producer prices rose 28.59% annually.
sections.whyItMatters: The 2.5% figure still shows growth, but the trajectory is deteriorating — industrial contraction and the 12.7% export drop reveal that the Iran war is no longer just an inflation problem; it is eating into real output. The CBRT's doubling of its inflation target signals the orthodox program is bending under pressure.

sections.heading: Manufacturing PMI nears stabilization as output returns to growth
sections.body:
In a counterpoint to the Q1 industrial contraction, Turkey's manufacturing sector neared stabilization in May, with the Istanbul Chamber of Industry PMI rising to its highest level since March 2024, according to Reuters. Output returned to growth and export orders rose for the first time in 21 months, suggesting the export collapse seen in Q1 data may be easing in the current quarter.

The improvement comes despite ongoing energy price volatility and the broader headwinds from the Iran war. The PMI data tracks May activity, whereas the Q1 GDP figures reflect January-March — a lag that may explain the divergence.
sections.whyItMatters: The first export order increase in 21 months is a cautiously positive signal that manufacturers are adapting to wartime trade disruptions. Whether this marks a genuine turn or a one-month bounce will depend on June data — and on whether the Hormuz blockade eases.

sections.heading: CHP in disarray as court-installed Kilicdaroglu returns to party headquarters
sections.body:
Turkey's main opposition CHP remains in deep crisis after court-installed leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu made his first visit to party headquarters on Saturday, three days after Eid, since a controversial court ruling scrapped the 2023 party primary. The 21 May ruling declared Ozgur Ozel's election as party leader marred by irregularities and restored Kilicdaroglu, who lost the 2023 presidential election.

On 24 May, riot police forced their way into CHP headquarters in Ankara, using pepper spray and batons to remove Ozel and his supporters, as reported by RFI and the Telegraph. At an Ankara rally, Ozel called for an urgent party congress, telling thousands: 'The CHP does not accept appointments.' Crowds chanted 'Traitor Kemal!' as Ozel challenged Kilicdaroglu to contest a party primary, saying he would give up the leadership if he received less than 85% support. Kilicdaroglu promised to 'bring a ballot box for party congress as soon as possible' without providing a date.

According to an opinion poll cited by RFI, only 11% of respondents approved of Ozel's removal. Gonul Tol of the Middle East Institute told RFI: 'This is the latest step in Erdogan's broader campaign to weaken the opposition,' linking it to the jailing of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu in March 2025. Tol predicted Turkey is 'moving closer to a Russia-style system where the leader decides who the opposition will be.' Political commentator Sezin Oney of Politikyol argued Erdogan wants to 'design the succession, most probably to have a close family member replacing him.'
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP crisis has moved from a legal ruling to a physical confrontation — police raiding opposition headquarters is a threshold event. With only 11% public support for the court's decision, the move may backfire politically, but the legal machinery is tightening around Ozel, with reports his parliamentary immunity could be stripped.

sections.heading: Erdogan leverages Iran war vacuum to consolidate power and expand defense exports
sections.body:
A Yahoo News analysis titled 'Erdogan is the real winner of Iran war' argues that the US-Iran conflict has given Erdogan room to accelerate democratic backsliding with minimal Western pushback. Gonul Tol of the Middle East Institute said: 'All those geopolitical shocks are allowing and emboldening Erdogan to do whatever he wants with whatever is left of the democratic space at home.' Yusuf Can of Amena Strategies noted: 'Erdogan's government understands that they don't have the popular support they used to have — that's why they are resorting to all these oppressive and authoritarian methods.'

At the same time, Turkey's defense export portfolio is expanding rapidly. Iraq signed a deal in May to buy 20 Turkish air defense systems, a significant shift for a country that has long relied on US air protection, according to the Telegraph. Indonesia signed a deal to buy up to 60 Bayraktar Kizilelma unmanned fighter aircraft, with delivery starting in 2028 — the first foreign buyer of the platform. Turkey is also exporting two military support ships to Portugal, the first such sale to a NATO and EU member state. Turkey is now the world's 11th largest arms exporter.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has created a dual dynamic: it gives Erdogan cover to crack down domestically while simultaneously boosting Turkey's defense-industrial appeal to countries losing confidence in US security guarantees. The Iraq air defense deal is especially notable — it marks a direct replacement of US security dependency with Turkish hardware.

sections.heading: Ankara unveils TANAP-style electricity corridor with Azerbaijan
sections.body:
Speaking at the opening of Baku Energy Week, Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar said Turkey, Azerbaijan, Georgia, Bulgaria and southeast European countries are working to strengthen regional energy connectivity. 'We are going to create the electricity version of TANAP,' Bayraktar said, referring to the Trans-Anatolian Natural Gas Pipeline. 'You will be hearing much more in the coming weeks and months about the project to carry the electricity resources to Turkey and, through Turkey, to Europe.'

The project could extend as far as Central Asia in the future. Turkey plans a $30 billion upgrade to its electricity transmission and distribution system over the next decade to accommodate higher renewable and nuclear output. Ankara also plans to upgrade electricity transmission connections with Georgia, Azerbaijan, and Bulgaria to trade surplus energy. President Erdogan said the proposed Green Electricity Transmission and Trade project linking the four countries would contribute to wider regional energy security. Bayraktar also reported progress on Turkmen gas transit via Azerbaijan: 'All of our counterparts are extremely interested. Perhaps in terms of timing, we have now reached a point where everyone will say yes.'
sections.whyItMatters: The TANAP-style electricity corridor is Ankara's answer to energy market disruption from the Hormuz blockade: if gas transit through the Strait is uncertain, electricity and pipeline gas from Azerbaijan and potentially Turkmenistan become more valuable. The $30 billion grid upgrade signals Turkey wants to position itself as an electricity transit hub, not just a gas one.

sections.heading: Turkey pushes for larger European defense role ahead of Ankara NATO Summit
sections.body:
Defense Minister Yasar Guler used a conference marking Turkey's 74th NATO anniversary to argue that Turkey is 'no longer a flank country' but 'a central ally capable of generating security across the entire European theater.' Guler criticized the EU's reluctance to open defense initiatives to Ankara and warned that marginalizing non-member allies 'would cause more harm to Europe's security and resilience than the reduction of US forces in Europe.' He announced Turkey will assume command of NATO's Allied Reaction Force from 2028 to 2030.

According to DefenseNews, Kadir Has University professor Serhat Guvenc noted that countries on NATO's eastern flank — Poland, Romania, the Baltics, Nordic allies — increasingly recognize Turkey's value. The Kurecik radar installation in central Turkey provides ballistic missile early warning coverage significantly deeper than Romania-based alternatives, said retired Brig. Gen. Huseyin Fazla of STRASAM: 'Without Kurecik, European ballistic missile defense against Iranian missiles would start from Romania — starting from Turkey provides substantially earlier warning and interception opportunity.'

However, Turkey's EU defense integration faces political obstacles: Greece and Cyprus block Turkish participation in PESCO and the European Defence Fund. Turkey's application to join the Military Mobility project has stalled. Fazla noted: 'Greece and Cyprus view Turkey's integration into EU defense architecture not as a contribution to their own security, but as the collapse of their strategy for balancing Turkey within the EU.'
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey is using the Iran war and the Trump-era reduction of US forces in Europe to leverage its way into European defense structures. The Kurecik radar point is concrete leverage — no European country can substitute for that early warning coverage. But the Greece-Cyprus veto remains a hard structural barrier that NATO summit diplomacy alone is unlikely to break.

sections.heading: Turkey lifts Armenia trade restriction; PKK peace process stalled by Iran war
sections.body:
Turkey removed a restriction on direct trade with Armenia last week, allowing shipments through third countries to list Turkey or Armenia as final destination or origin, AP reported. Foreign Ministry spokesman Oncu Keceli said technical work toward opening the shared border continues. Armenia welcomed the move as 'an important step toward the establishment of full and normalized relations.' The border has been closed since 1993.

Meanwhile, the PKK peace process remains near standstill. Reuters reported that Iran war fallout has brought Turkey's peace process with PKK militants 'to near standstill'. Kurdish militants want guarantees before disarming; Ankara insists on full disarmament first. Both sides are hesitant to take next steps amid regional instability. The PKK formally dissolved in May 2025, but implementation has stalled as the war environment makes both guarantees and verification harder. Turkey's pro-Kurdish DEM party has warned that violence against Kurdish groups in Syria risks undermining fragile reconciliation efforts.
sections.whyItMatters: The Armenia trade move is a small but symbolically important step in Turkey's neighborhood diplomacy — part of a pattern of quiet normalization with old rivals. The PKK stall is more consequential: the Iran war has frozen a process that was supposed to end a 40-year insurgency, and there is no clear path to unfreezing it while regional instability persists.

bottomLine: June 1 brings a mixed picture: Turkey's economy is still growing but decelerating, with Q1 data confirming the Iran war's bite into exports and industry, while May PMI offers a glimmer of manufacturing stabilization. Domestically, the CHP crisis has entered a more volatile phase after police stormed opposition headquarters, and analysts see Erdogan using wartime distraction to consolidate power. On the external front, Ankara is making strategic bets — a TANAP-style electricity corridor with Azerbaijan, defense exports to Iraq and Indonesia, and a push for deeper European defense integration — all aimed at turning wartime disruption into geopolitical opportunity. The PKK peace process, by contrast, remains frozen by the very same instability Ankara is trying to exploit.
sources: Daily Sabah, Reuters, RFI, AP News, Forbes (Guney Yildiz), DefenseNews, Yahoo News / Telegraph, Middle East Institute (Gonul Tol), STRASAM (Huseyin Fazla), Kadir Has University (Serhat Guvenc), Amena Strategies (Yusuf Can), ING, TurkStat, Politikyol (Sezin Oney)
